{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":4,"markdown":"# Summary of Completeness Trends (July 2023 – June 2025)

Overall completeness: Most indicators consistently exceeded 95% completeness.

Areas of weaker completeness: Completeness was lower for family planning methods (~70–85%) and for severe malnutrition indicators (60–75%), where reporting gaps and 0 values were more common.

Improvement in 2025: Modest gains in weaker areas, but generally sustained high completeness for most indicators, supporting confidence that observed service use reflects true trends rather than reporting gaps.

# Why Completeness Matters for the Disruptions Analysis

Observed values: These are adjusted for outliers only, so they reflect the actual raw service volumes after removing implausible spikes.

Expected values: These are adjusted for both completeness and outliers. This means the model “fills in” where reporting gaps exist, building an expected trend line as if all facilities had reported consistently.

When completeness is high, observed and expected volumes are more comparable, and disruptions are more likely to reflect true service changes.

When completeness is low, expected values may be artificially higher than observed, creating apparent “disruptions” that actually reflect missing reports rather than real declines in service delivery.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.64,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.6,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"61448ef4-8784-43af-9f96-ad5897a53190","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Completeness is >95% for most indicators in 2025, strengthening confidence in disruption findings"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Methodology: Service Utilization Assessment","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Purpose: 
Track changes in health service use over time, identifying where services fall below or rise above expected patterns.

# How it works:
- Uses routine HMIS data, cleaned for outliers and missing values.
- Builds an “expected” trend line for each service, adjusting for seasonality and historical trends in service utilization.
- Compares actual service volumes to expected levels.


# Measuring impact:
- Flagged disruption periods are analyzed to estimate how much service volumes changed compared to what was expected.
- Results are shown at nationaland sub-national levels highlighting both system-wide and localized effects.


# How to interpret figures
- Red shaded areas = potential disruptions (service volumes lower than expected).
- Green shaded areas = potential surpluses (service volumes higher than expected).
- These are signals, not conclusions — they highlight when and where volumes deviate, but require further investigation into the underlying reasons (“why”)."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found alongside the source code on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"span":3,"markdown":"# Overall Performance

Nationally, data completeness averaged 89%, indicating generally strong but uneven reporting across indicators and regions.

High completeness (≥90%) was sustained for most maternal and child health indicators, including ANC1, Penta1, Penta3, and outpatient visits, showing consistent DHIMS2 data entry and validation.

However, performance declines for BCG vaccination, institutional delivery, postnatal care, and adolescent family planning, exposing programmatic and data management gaps.

# Low-Performing Regions and Indicators

Eastern, Upper West, and Greater Accra show persistent low completeness (<80%) for institutional delivery, postnatal care, and BCG, likely linked to fragmented reporting and inconsistent follow-up."},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.7,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"c20f1672-edfc-4140-ae2c-09a30b50443a","moduleId":"m001","selectedReplicantValue":""},"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":true}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Completeness of Reported Data, DHIMS2"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"b83cd81d-efcd-44d8-9efd-d8e7d4825cd4","moduleId":"m002","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Percent change in RMNCAH-N service volume due to completeness adjustment"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"eebe45e3-d605-4899-913a-9c715abae2de","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}]],"useFooter":true,"footerLogos":["MOH-logo.png"]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":4,"markdown":"# Summary of Completeness Trends (July 2023 – June 2025)

Overall completeness: Most indicators consistently exceeded 90 % completeness, with several maintaining ≥95 % since early 2024.

Areas of weaker completeness: Completeness was lower in 2023, particularly for BCG (65–75 %) and ANC4 and PNC1 (around 80–85 %), but improved steadily through 2024 and remained above 85–90 % for nearly all indicators by mid-2025. Family planning and severe malnutrition indicators (not shown) remain the weakest areas, averaging around 70–85 %.

Improvement in 2025: Reporting completeness increased further in 2025, with most indicators maintaining ≥90 % completeness each month, supporting confidence that observed service volumes reflect true utilization patterns rather than reporting gaps.

# Why Completeness Matters for the Disruptions Analysis
In Ghana, data was adjusted for this analysis as follows:
- Observed values: These are adjusted for outliers only, so they reflect the actual raw service volumes after removing implausible spikes.

- Expected values: These are adjusted for both completeness and outliers. This means the model “fills in” where reporting gaps exist, building an expected trend line as if all facilities had reported consistently.

When completeness is high, observed and expected volumes are more comparable, and disruptions are more likely to reflect true service changes.

When completeness is low (like in BCG), expected values may be artificially higher than observed, creating apparent “disruptions” that actually reflect missing reports rather than real declines in service delivery.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.64,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.6,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"a08cb7b8-02b6-42e8-bb51-5380cbe7df7c","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Completeness is >95% for most indicators in 2025, strengthening confidence in disruption findings"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"The review of RMNCAH-N service delivery from 2020 to September 2025 demonstrates a health system that has largely sustained access to essential maternal and newborn care, with overall utilisation remaining aligned to expected demand trends. ANC1 coverage has remained consistently strong throughout the period, reflecting stable first contact with the health system and continued community confidence in accessing antenatal services. ANC4, which requires ongoing engagement and often reveals system strain earlier, similarly tracked expected seasonal and annual patterns, suggesting that once women enter the antenatal care pathway, most are able to return for subsequent visits. This continuity signals resilience in both health-seeking behaviour and delivery of routine maternal care services.

Delivery services show a parallel pattern of resilience, with skilled birth attendance levels demonstrating sustained use across the five years. Periodic peaks observed in late 2024 and early 2025 may reflect improvements in reporting timeliness or enhanced referral effectiveness, while slight dips indicate transient disruptions rather than systemic declines. Postnatal care utilisation further mirrors this trajectory, maintaining steady contact between new mothers and the health system during the critical postpartum period. Live births reported through the system also follow similar patterns, reinforcing that women remained able to give birth within facilities and that service access has been protected.

While utilisation across the maternal care continuum appears stable, the findings draw attention to areas requiring policy and programmatic attention. Persistent moderate-to-severe anaemia among pregnant women continues to place mothers at heightened risk of complications, revealing treatment and preventive supplementation gaps that must be addressed. Data completeness challenges persist across some key indicators, particularly around delivery, which impede timely detection of deteriorations and reduce confidence in sub-national comparisons. Mild, emerging disruptions in certain services in 2025 raise the possibility that constraints in health worker availability, commodity stock-outs, and referral capacity may be beginning to influence service efficiency and patient experience.

Taken together, the trends provide assurance that maternal health service utilisation remains robust, with no evidence of broad declines in women accessing ANC, delivery care, or postnatal services in 2024–2025. However, service use alone cannot confirm that quality of care has been preserved. Supply-side conditions — including available midwifery workforce, essential medicines (especially haematinics), diagnostic supplies, and emergency obstetric readiness — may have been affected by fiscal tightening and changes in financing flows. These dimensions are not visible in DHIMS2 utilisation data and therefore require dedicated follow-up assessment. Upper West (70.9%) and Eastern (77.8%) fall below the expected minimum, indicating under-reporting of even core delivery outcomes. Completeness declines further for mild anaemia at registration, where all regions fall below 80%, ranging from 63.0% in Upper West to 77.4% in Northern. The situation worsens by 36 weeks, with reporting dropping into the low 50–60% range nationally, Eastern (51.7%), Western North (50.7%), and Upper West (49.5%) illustrate the weakest continuity of reporting as pregnancy advances. Severe anaemia indicators reveal the lowest completeness of all, with most regions reporting fewer than 20% of expected data at 36 weeks, Central at 17.0%, Bono at 16.5%, and Savannah at 16.1%. Even at registration, only Northern (32.5%) and North East (34.5%) exceed 30%. These patterns suggest that while deliveries are relatively well-captured, anaemia surveillance, particularly late-pregnancy risks, is largely missing in the current dataset.","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Completeness of Reported  RMNCAH-N Data on DHIMS2 "}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":4,"markdown":"# Summary of Completeness Trends (July 2023 – June 2025)

Overall completeness: Most indicators consistently exceeded 90 % completeness, with several maintaining ≥95 % since early 2024.

Areas of weaker completeness: Completeness was lower in 2023, particularly for BCG (65–75 %) and ANC4 and PNC1 (around 80–85 %), but improved steadily through 2024 and remained above 85–90 % for nearly all indicators by mid-2025. Family planning and severe malnutrition indicators (not shown) remain the weakest areas, averaging around 70–85 %.

Improvement in 2025: Reporting completeness increased further in 2025, with most indicators maintaining ≥90 % completeness each month, supporting confidence that observed service volumes reflect true utilization patterns rather than reporting gaps.

# Why Completeness Matters for the Disruptions Analysis
In Ghana, data was adjusted for this analysis as follows:
- Observed values: These are adjusted for outliers only, so they reflect the actual raw service volumes after removing implausible spikes.

- Expected values: These are adjusted for both completeness and outliers. This means the model “fills in” where reporting gaps exist, building an expected trend line as if all facilities had reported consistently.

When completeness is high, observed and expected volumes are more comparable, and disruptions are more likely to reflect true service changes.

When completeness is low (like in BCG), expected values may be artificially higher than observed, creating apparent “disruptions” that actually reflect missing reports rather than real declines in service delivery.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.64,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.6,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"a08cb7b8-02b6-42e8-bb51-5380cbe7df7c","moduleId":"m001","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Completeness is >95% for most indicators in 2025, strengthening confidence in disruption findings"}}

{"type":"cover","cover":{"titleText":"RMNCAH-N Service Utilization and Key\nData Quality Gaps","titleTextRelFontSize":7,"subTitleText":"Jan 2020 - Sept 2025","subTitleTextRelFontSize":4,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":["FASTR_Primary_01_FullName.png","ghs-logo.png"]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"placeholder","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"The review of RMNCAH-N service delivery from 2020 to September 2025 demonstrates a health system that has largely sustained access to essential maternal and newborn care, with overall utilisation remaining aligned to expected demand trends. ANC1 coverage has remained consistently strong throughout the period, reflecting stable first contact with the health system and continued community confidence in accessing antenatal services. ANC4, which requires ongoing engagement and often reveals system strain earlier, similarly tracked expected seasonal and annual patterns, suggesting that once women enter the antenatal care pathway, most are able to return for subsequent visits. This continuity signals resilience in both health-seeking behaviour and delivery of routine maternal care services.

Delivery services show a parallel pattern of resilience, with skilled birth attendance levels demonstrating sustained use across the five years. Periodic peaks observed in late 2024 and early 2025 may reflect improvements in reporting timeliness or enhanced referral effectiveness, while slight dips indicate transient disruptions rather than systemic declines. Postnatal care utilisation further mirrors this trajectory, maintaining steady contact between new mothers and the health system during the critical postpartum period. Live births reported through the system also follow similar patterns, reinforcing that women remained able to give birth within facilities and that service access has been protected.

While utilisation across the maternal care continuum appears stable, the findings draw attention to areas requiring policy and programmatic attention. Persistent moderate-to-severe anaemia among pregnant women continues to place mothers at heightened risk of complications, revealing treatment and preventive supplementation gaps that must be addressed. Data completeness challenges persist across some key indicators, particularly around delivery, which impede timely detection of deteriorations and reduce confidence in sub-national comparisons. Mild, emerging disruptions in certain services in 2025 raise the possibility that constraints in health worker availability, commodity stock-outs, and referral capacity may be beginning to influence service efficiency and patient experience.

Taken together, the trends provide assurance that maternal health service utilisation remains robust, with no evidence of broad declines in women accessing ANC, delivery care, or postnatal services in 2024–2025. However, service use alone cannot confirm that quality of care has been preserved. Supply-side conditions — including available midwifery workforce, essential medicines (especially haematinics), diagnostic supplies, and emergency obstetric readiness — may have been affected by fiscal tightening and changes in financing flows. These dimensions are not visible in DHIMS2 utilisation data and therefore require dedicated follow-up assessment. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"span":3,"markdown":"# Overall Overview

Internal consistency across key maternal and child health indicators remains uneven, with stronger alignment in antenatal data and weaker coherence in delivery and immunization data.

Nationally, 86% of sub-national areas met ANC consistency benchmarks (ANC1 > ANC4) — reflecting solid continuity in antenatal reporting.

However, only 27% achieved alignment between delivery and BCG data, and 59% between Penta1 and Penta3, suggesting fragmented data linkages between service delivery points.

# Regional Patterns

High performers: North East, Savannah, Western, and Oti consistently demonstrate strong data coherence across indicators.

Low performers: Greater Accra and Ahafo show inconsistencies across all indicators"},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"cf5b8649-93c2-4bbe-8f2d-773f42ce8ec3","moduleId":"m001","selectedReplicantValue":""},"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Internal consistency of Reported Data"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Disruptions and Surpluses in ANC and Delivery Services
Across the four key indicators, Antenatal Care 1 (ANC1), Antenatal Care 4 (ANC4), Institutional Delivery, and Live Births, service volumes generally follow expected seasonal patterns, with only short periods of deviation. The trends show that the system maintained stability over most of the 2020–2025 period, with disruptions or surpluses appearing in isolated quarters rather than sustained shifts.

Antenatal Care 1: ANC1 remained mostly aligned with expected levels throughout the period. Small surpluses are visible in Q3–Q4 2020, suggesting stronger-than-usual service uptake during that period. Mild disruptions emerge in Q3–Q4 2022, where observed volumes dipped slightly below expected levels. From late 2023 to early 2025, ANC1 returns to a stable pattern, ending with slight surpluses in Q2–Q3 2025.

Antenatal Care 4: ANC4 shows a similar pattern of stability with minimal deviations. 
"}],[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Disruptions and Surpluses in Anaemia and Haemoglobin-Related Service Volumes

Across the four indicators, hemoglobin testing at ≥36 weeks, haemoglobin testing at ANC registration, mild anaemia at registration, and mild anaemia at 36 weeks, service volumes remain highly consistent with expected trends, with only brief deviations.

Hemoglobin checked at ≥36 weeks of pregnancy: Service volumes follow the expected trajectory closely. A noticeable surplus appears in Q2–Q3 2025, where testing rises above predicted levels. 

Hemoglobin checked at ANC registration: Testing at first contact is similarly stable. Small surpluses occur around Q3–Q4 2020 and again Q2–Q3 2025, suggesting periods of improved ANC screening completeness.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Testing and Anaemia status at Registration
Across the period from 2020 to 2025, haemoglobin testing at ANC registration remained consistently high, generally ranging between 210,000 and 250,000 tests per quarter, indicating strong adherence to ANC screening protocols.

Among those tested, mild to moderate anaemia at registration has remained the dominant burden, typically ranging between 85,000 and 100,000 cases per quarter. This translates to an estimated 35%–45% of all women tested being mildly anaemic in most quarters. The pattern is relatively stable across the five-year period, with only small fluctuations that likely reflect seasonal changes and district-level variations in ANC attendance.

Severe anaemia at registration, although much less common, remains persistent. Severe cases generally ranged between 8,000 and 13,000 per quarter, representing roughly 3%–6% of all women tested. The proportion of severe anaemia shows a slow downward drift after 2022, with the lowest proportions observed in mid-2025, suggesting modest but meaningful improvement in early identification and treatment of high-risk pregnancies.

The combined trend suggests that while haemoglobin testing coverage is being maintained, anaemia remains a significant clinical burden, particularly in the mild category. Persistent severe anaemia cases- though smaller in proportion -continue to highlight gaps in nutritional supplementation and early ANC uptake. Strengthening iron–folate adherence, malaria prevention in pregnancy, and timely ANC initiation can be essential to further reduce the proportion of severe cases."}],[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Testing and Anaemia Status at 36 weeks
Haemoglobin testing at ≥36 weeks of pregnancy remained high across the period, generally ranging between 110,000 and 145,000 tests per quarter. Although this indicates continuity in late pregnancy, testing at 36 weeks remains much lower than at registration, indicating gaps at follow-up testing in late pregnancy. 

Mild to moderate anaemia at 36 weeks consistently accounted for the majority of anaemia detected late in pregnancy. Across the years, it ranged between 42,000 and 52,000 cases per quarter, representing approximately 35%–40% of all women tested. The trend shows a gentle rise from 2021 to 2023 before stabilizing around the mid-40,000s in 2024–2025.

Severe anaemia at 36 weeks remained low in absolute numbers, generally between 5,000 and 8,000 cases per quarter. This corresponds to roughly 4%–6% of all women tested. The proportion peaks around 2021–2022, then gradually decreases through 2024–2025, suggesting small but encouraging improvements in maternal iron status and management of conditions that drive severe anaemia (e.g., malaria in pregnancy, late ANC initiation, nutritional deficiencies).","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"success","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":false,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Hemoglobin Checking and Anaemia Status"}}

Afterward, a mild downward fluctuation is seen in 2023–2024, followed by a sharper decline in early 2025 before modest recovery by Q3 2025.\n\nThroughout the series, the number of institutional deliveries remained only marginally below live births — typically within a 2–3% difference — implying that most births occurred in health facilities. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# What is data quality assessment?
The Data Quality Assessment (DQA) methodology applies three core assessments: detection of extreme outliers in reported service volumes, analysis of indicator-specific completeness, and consistency checks between related indicators. For each facility and month, the data are processed to identify anomalies, assess reporting continuity, and validate internal coherence across indicators. Outliers are flagged using Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) methods and proportional thresholds. Completeness is evaluated based on whether facilities report non-zero values across months for core indicators. Consistency is checked across linked indicators (e.g., ANC1 vs ANC4, Penta1 vs Penta3), using predefined ratio thresholds. The results are integrated into a composite DQA score that summarizes whether a facility's data meet all quality benchmarks.

# What is data quality adjustment? 
Data quality adjustment is the process of correcting known issues in routine health data to improve the reliability of analyses. In the FASTR approach, adjustments are applied to HMIS data using automated methods that identify and correct outlier values and impute missing data based on patterns in historical reporting. This allows countries to make more robust use of the data they already have, even when reporting is imperfect.

# Why adjust for data quality?
Routine health data, such as those captured in DHIS2, are an essential resource for monitoring and improving primary health care systems. However, data quality issues — like outliers, incomplete reporting, and inconsistencies between related indicators — can distort analyses and misinform decision-making. 
The FASTR approach includes built-in statistical methods to identify and adjust for these issues, producing more robust and actionable results. While not all data quality challenges can be fully resolved, targeted adjustments improve the reliability of the data and help ensure it can still inform decisions. Rather than treating poor data quality as a barrier, FASTR transforms it into an opportunity for feedback and improvement, enabling countries to use the data they have to make timely, evidence-based decisions.

# How was adjustment done?
The Data Quality Adjustment module uses HMIS data together with the outlier and completeness flags generated by the Data Quality Assessment module, which identify where service volumes are unusually high or missing. Outlier values are replaced with a centered 12-month facility-level rolling average based on valid (non-flagged) data. Missing values due to incomplete reporting are imputed using the same method. If there is not enough valid historical data, a fallback average at the facility level is used instead. Adjustments are applied under four scenarios - no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and both combined - to enable sensitivity analysis. Indicators which don't meet minimum volume thresholds are excluded from adjustment.
For this analysis, adjustment is for outliers and completeness."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology: Data Quality Adjustment Approach"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"Analysis of routine health management information systems data (HMIS) and the methodological choices contained in this report provide strengths and limitations which are important to consider in the interpretation of the results."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Strengths 
- Leverages routinely collected HMIS data, enabling timely and low-cost monitoring.
- Includes rigorous data quality assessment and adjustment to improve reliability of estimates.
- Enables subnational analysis for granular insights.
- Supports policy-relevant questions through tailored indicator selection and flexible stratification.
- Accounts for seasonality and historical trends using statistical methods not available in DHIS2.
- Complements existing DHIS2 tools and strengthens data use through feedback loops."},{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Limitations
- Cannot correct for all types of data quality issues (e.g., systematic underreporting, denominator inaccuracies).
- Does not account for facilities that never report, which may bias results in areas with large reporting gaps.
- Low-volume indicators are more sensitive to anomalies and may not be adjusted.
- Requires consistent indicator definitions and stable reporting systems over time for trend analysis."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Strengths and limitations of this analysis"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Disruptions and Surpluses in ANC and Delivery Services
Across the four key indicators, Antenatal Care 1 (ANC1), Antenatal Care 4 (ANC4), Institutional Delivery, and Live Births, service volumes generally follow expected seasonal patterns, with only short periods of deviation. The trends show that the system maintained stability over most of the 2020–2025 period, with disruptions or surpluses appearing in isolated quarters rather than sustained shifts.

Antenatal Care 1: ANC1 remained mostly aligned with expected levels throughout the period. Small surpluses are visible in Q3–Q4 2020, suggesting stronger-than-usual service uptake during that period. Mild disruptions emerge in Q3–Q4 2022, where observed volumes dipped slightly below expected levels. From late 2023 to early 2025, ANC1 returns to a stable pattern, ending with slight surpluses in Q2–Q3 2025.

Antenatal Care 4: ANC4 shows a similar pattern of stability with minimal deviations. 
"}],[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Disruptions and Surpluses in Anaemia and Haemoglobin-Related Service Volumes

Across the four indicators, hemoglobin testing at ≥36 weeks, haemoglobin testing at ANC registration, mild anaemia at registration, and mild anaemia at 36 weeks, service volumes remain highly consistent with expected trends, with only brief deviations.

Hemoglobin checked at ≥36 weeks of pregnancy: Service volumes follow the expected trajectory closely. A noticeable surplus appears in Q2–Q3 2025, where testing rises above predicted levels. 

Hemoglobin checked at ANC registration: Testing at first contact is similarly stable. Small surpluses occur around Q3–Q4 2020 and again Q2–Q3 2025, suggesting periods of improved ANC screening completeness.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":5,"markdown":"Data completeness for RMNCAH-N indicators on DHIMS2 shows wide variation and major gaps, especially for anaemia monitoring. Only one region, Ahafo, achieves ≥90% completeness for live births (90.1%), while most regions report between 82–88%. Upper West (70.9%) and Eastern (77.8%) fall below the expected minimum, indicating under-reporting of even core delivery outcomes. Completeness declines further for mild anaemia at registration, where all regions fall below 80%, ranging from 63.0% in Upper West to 77.4% in Northern. The situation worsens by 36 weeks, with reporting dropping into the low 50–60% range nationally, Eastern (51.7%), Western North (50.7%), and Upper West (49.5%) illustrate the weakest continuity of reporting as pregnancy advances. Severe anaemia indicators reveal the lowest completeness of all, with most regions reporting fewer than 20% of expected data at 36 weeks, Central at 17.0%, Bono at 16.5%, and Savannah at 16.1%. Even at registration, only Northern (32.5%) and North East (34.5%) exceed 30%. These patterns suggest that while deliveries are relatively well-captured, anaemia surveillance, particularly late-pregnancy risks, is largely missing in the current dataset.","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Completeness of Reported  RMNCAH-N Data on DHIMS2 "}}

{"type":"section","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"Annex 2: Methodology","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"placeholder","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1}]],"useHeader":true}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# What is data quality assessment?
The Data Quality Assessment (DQA) methodology applies three core assessments: detection of extreme outliers in reported service volumes, analysis of indicator-specific completeness, and consistency checks between related indicators. For each facility and month, the data are processed to identify anomalies, assess reporting continuity, and validate internal coherence across indicators. Outliers are flagged using Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) methods and proportional thresholds. Completeness is evaluated based on whether facilities report non-zero values across months for core indicators. Consistency is checked across linked indicators (e.g., ANC1 vs ANC4, Penta1 vs Penta3), using predefined ratio thresholds. The results are integrated into a composite DQA score that summarizes whether a facility's data meet all quality benchmarks.

# What is data quality adjustment? 
Data quality adjustment is the process of correcting known issues in routine health data to improve the reliability of analyses. In the FASTR approach, adjustments are applied to HMIS data using automated methods that identify and correct outlier values and impute missing data based on patterns in historical reporting. This allows countries to make more robust use of the data they already have, even when reporting is imperfect.

# Why adjust for data quality?
Routine health data, such as those captured in DHIS2, are an essential resource for monitoring and improving primary health care systems. However, data quality issues — like outliers, incomplete reporting, and inconsistencies between related indicators — can distort analyses and misinform decision-making. 
The FASTR approach includes built-in statistical methods to identify and adjust for these issues, producing more robust and actionable results. While not all data quality challenges can be fully resolved, targeted adjustments improve the reliability of the data and help ensure it can still inform decisions. Rather than treating poor data quality as a barrier, FASTR transforms it into an opportunity for feedback and improvement, enabling countries to use the data they have to make timely, evidence-based decisions.

# How was adjustment done?
The Data Quality Adjustment module uses HMIS data together with the outlier and completeness flags generated by the Data Quality Assessment module, which identify where service volumes are unusually high or missing. Outlier values are replaced with a centered 12-month facility-level rolling average based on valid (non-flagged) data. Missing values due to incomplete reporting are imputed using the same method. If there is not enough valid historical data, a fallback average at the facility level is used instead. Adjustments are applied under four scenarios - no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and both combined - to enable sensitivity analysis. Indicators which don't meet minimum volume thresholds are excluded from adjustment.
For this analysis, adjustment is for outliers and completeness."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology: Data Quality Adjustment Approach"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Overview of the Data Quality Assessment Methodology 
Identifies key data quality issues by assessing indicator completeness, detecting extreme outliers, and evaluating consistency between related indicators — using monthly, facility-level HMIS data.

Applies targeted adjustments to flagged data points by replacing outliers and imputing missing values using a centered 12-month rolling average; fallback facility-level averages are used when insufficient history exists.

Enables sensitivity analysis by producing results under four scenarios (no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and combined), with certain indicators that don't meet inclusion criteria excluded from adjustment. For this analysis, adjustment is for both outliers and completeness.

# Overview of the Service Utilization Assessment Methodology 
Service utilization trend analysis, which identifies the percentage change in service use for each quarter of data compared to the previous quarter.

Disruptions and surplus in service delivery utilization analysis, which helps to identify meaningful positive or negative changes in service use outside of what we would expect given seasonal trends and historical trends.

# Overview of the Service Coverage Estimation Methodology
Coverage estimation analysis uses routine data to estimate trends in service coverage at national and sub-national levels. This occurs by integrating adjusted health service volume data, population projections, and survey data (MICS/DHS). Coverage estimates are calculated for key health indicators using multiple denominator sources, and the optimal denominator is selected by minimizing the error relative to the most recent survey data."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found in the Annex. All R code and source documentation is also publicly available on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"c772ef64-3b3d-4131-ad33-22dfc904d51f","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}],[{"type":"text","markdown":"The comparison of haemoglobin testing at ANC registration versus testing at ≥36 weeks shows a consistent gap in coverage, revealing weaknesses in sustained antenatal follow-up and late-pregnancy anaemia surveillance. While annual haemoglobin checks at registration remained high, ranging from about 914,000 women in 2020 to 970,582 in 2022, the number checked again near term was roughly 40–50% lower in every year of the review. For example, in 2022, 970,582 women were tested at registration but only 554,230 were tested again at 36 weeks — leaving over 416,000 pregnant women without late-stage haemoglobin monitoring that year. In 2025, both indicators show a steep decline: haemoglobin testing at registration dropped to 487,243 — almost half of the 2024 value (922,829) — while late-pregnancy testing dropped even further to 280,152. The scale of this reduction suggests a systemwide disruption affecting laboratory verification or follow-up contact, rather than reduced ANC attendance alone. This widening gap indicates that many women are being screened once during pregnancy but not monitored again when anaemia risk peaks, reducing the ability of the health system to detect and manage preventable maternal complications such as haemorrhage and low-birthweight risk.","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Comparing Haemoglobin Checking at Registration and 36 weeks"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1.25,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Disruptions in Child Health Service Volumes (Jan 2024–Sep 2025)

# Pre-withdrawal (2024)
Most indicators, including Penta 1, Penta 3, and Polio vaccines, show relative stability, though BCG experienced mild dips in mid-2024, likely reflecting routine operational fluctuations rather than systemic decline.

# Immediately after withdrawal (Q1–Q2 2025)
Instead of sharp declines, service volumes for most indicators either stabilized or slightly rebounded. For example, Penta 1 and Polio 3 show modest gains, suggesting that GHS mechanisms and other partners may have absorbed parts of the funding and logistical gap.

# Isolated vulnerabilities:

BCG shows small contractions in Q3 2025, hinting at delayed effects, possibly linked to procurement or cold-chain funding gaps. Vitamin A supplementation remained stable, likely due to continued campaign-based delivery and alternate funding streams (e.g., UNICEF).

# Summary:
There’s no evidence of an abrupt or widespread collapse in service volumes post-USAID exit, though minor slowdowns in some immunization indicators suggest early signs of reduced system elasticity, a potential early warning that sustained funding shortfalls could erode service delivery over time if not mitigated."}]]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1.25,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Key Observation

Across all indicators, antenatal care (ANC 1 & 4), institutional delivery, postnatal care, BCG vaccination, and early breastfeeding initiation — no major national-level disruption is evident immediately following USAID’s withdrawal in early 2025.

Instead, most indicators show a short-term rebound or stabilization in service volumes between Q1 and Q2 2025, suggesting that Ghana Health Service systems largely maintained continuity of essential services.

# Detailed Insights

Maternal health indicators (ANC 1, ANC 4, Institutional delivery, PNC1): Show small dips in late 2024 (Q3–Q4) followed by modest recoveries in early 2025. ANC1 volumes through 2025 remained slightly above expected levels (~7 %). ANC4 stayed close to trend in 2025, averaging ~70 000 monthly vs. ~68 600 expected (~2 % higher). These trends could be indicator of resilience and local system adaptation.

Child health indicators (BCG, Early breastfeeding): Show mild fluctuations, but no sustained declines after Q1 2025; this suggests continuity of routine service delivery and possibly compensatory domestic funding. However, early breastfeeding is about 10-12 % lower than deliveries, highlighting a modest continuity gap after childbirth.

No new disruptions appear after Q1 2025 — unlike the temporary service contraction observed mid-2024."}]]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Methodology: Service Utilization Assessment","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Purpose: 
Track changes in health service use over time, identifying where services fall below or rise above expected patterns.

# How it works:
- Uses routine HMIS data, cleaned for outliers and missing values.
- Builds an “expected” trend line for each service, adjusting for seasonality and historical trends in service utilization.
- Compares actual service volumes to expected levels.


# Measuring impact:
- Flagged disruption periods are analyzed to estimate how much service volumes changed compared to what was expected.
- Results are shown at nationaland sub-national levels highlighting both system-wide and localized effects.


# How to interpret figures
- Red shaded areas = potential disruptions (service volumes lower than expected).
- Green shaded areas = potential surpluses (service volumes higher than expected).
- These are signals, not conclusions — they highlight when and where volumes deviate, but require further investigation into the underlying reasons (“why”)."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found alongside the source code on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":false,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

- Weakest in laboratory testing compliance

{"type":"cover","cover":{"titleText":"RMNCAH-N Service Utilization and Key\nData Quality Gaps","titleTextRelFontSize":7,"subTitleText":"Jan 2020 - Sept 2025","subTitleTextRelFontSize":4,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":["FASTR_Primary_01_FullName.png","ghs-logo.png"]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"placeholder","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Overview of the Data Quality Assessment Methodology 
Identifies key data quality issues by assessing indicator completeness, detecting extreme outliers, and evaluating consistency between related indicators — using monthly, facility-level HMIS data.

Applies targeted adjustments to flagged data points by replacing outliers and imputing missing values using a centered 12-month rolling average; fallback facility-level averages are used when insufficient history exists.

Enables sensitivity analysis by producing results under four scenarios (no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and combined), with certain indicators that don't meet inclusion criteria excluded from adjustment. For this analysis, adjustment is for both outliers and completeness.

# Overview of the Service Utilization Assessment Methodology 
Service utilization trend analysis, which identifies the percentage change in service use for each quarter of data compared to the previous quarter.

Disruptions and surplus in service delivery utilization analysis, which helps to identify meaningful positive or negative changes in service use outside of what we would expect given seasonal trends and historical trends.

# Overview of the Service Coverage Estimation Methodology
Coverage estimation analysis uses routine data to estimate trends in service coverage at national and sub-national levels. This occurs by integrating adjusted health service volume data, population projections, and survey data (MICS/DHS). Coverage estimates are calculated for key health indicators using multiple denominator sources, and the optimal denominator is selected by minimizing the error relative to the most recent survey data."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"All R code and source documentation is publicly available on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","span":3,"markdown":"All three indicators—facility deliveries, PNC1 (mothers), and breastfeeding within 30 minutes—follow the same pattern: modest fluctuations through 2024, a dip late 2024, then a sharp rebound in early 2025 to peak levels by mid-year. \n\nThe level of service delivery is similar across the three indicators, signaling adequate continuity of care.","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false},{"type":"figure","presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"8db720a7-35e9-4f2e-8d71-2d2f8d75fd18","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""},"stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Trends in deliveries and postnatal care services","useFooter":false,"footerText":""}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"image","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgFile":"23368207381_d50741e3e9_o.jpg","imgFit":"cover","markdown":"","imgStretch":true,"imgHeight":250},{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1.25,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"The FASTR approach uses routine HMIS data to monitor how service delivery shifts over time.

Findings generate actionable evidence to guide rapid responses, helping sustain continuity of essential care during funding uncertainty or operational change."}],[{"type":"placeholder","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Tracking Disruptions in Essential Services Using HMIS Data","useFooter":false,"footerLogos":[]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"2e9e2795-d7bd-47a9-a8e8-cd117a7312c5","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}],[{"type":"text","markdown":"The trend of reported maternal deaths from Q1 2020 to Q3 2025 shows a clear rise followed by a gradual decline:

Upward trend (2020 – 2022): Maternal deaths increased sharply from 532 in Q1 2020 to a peak of 920 – 929 between Q4 2022 and Q2 2023, indicating a worsening pattern through the pandemic and early post-pandemic recovery period.

Peak stability (2022 – 2023): The plateau around 900 deaths per quarter in 2022–2023 suggests sustained high mortality, possibly linked to increased service utilization (more deliveries in facilities) combined with health system strain or reporting improvements.

Gradual decline (2024 – 2025): Deaths began to drop steadily from Q4 2023 (889) to Q2 2025 (683), with a slight uptick to 724 in Q3 2025. This overall 20–25% reduction from the 2022–2023 peak points to gradual improvements in maternal outcomes, likely from strengthened facility readiness and better quality of care.","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"success","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":false,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]]}}

Clear performance gradient:
- **ANC 1 visits: 84-95%** (best)
- **ANC 4 visits: 76-94%** (good)
- **Hb at registration: 80-94%** (moderate)
- **Hb at ≥36 weeks: 64-87%** (poorest)

### 3. **Service Delivery vs. Clinical Quality Gap**
Facilities report patient visits better than clinical procedures, indicating:
- Stronger administrative systems than clinical documentation
- Possible actual service delivery gaps, not just reporting issues

## Recommendations for Health System Management

**Immediate Actions:**
1. **Emergency intervention** for Savannah's late-pregnancy Hb testing (63.6%)
2. **Targeted support** for Eastern region across all indicators
3. **Laboratory strengthening** program for northern regions

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Testing and Anaemia status at Registration
Across the period from 2020 to 2025, haemoglobin testing at ANC registration remained consistently high, generally ranging between 210,000 and 250,000 tests per quarter, indicating strong adherence to ANC screening protocols.

Among those tested, mild to moderate anaemia at registration has remained the dominant burden, typically ranging between 85,000 and 100,000 cases per quarter. This translates to an estimated 35%–45% of all women tested being mildly anaemic in most quarters. The pattern is relatively stable across the five-year period, with only small fluctuations that likely reflect seasonal changes and district-level variations in ANC attendance.

Severe anaemia at registration, although much less common, remains persistent. Severe cases generally ranged between 8,000 and 13,000 per quarter, representing roughly 3%–6% of all women tested. The proportion of severe anaemia shows a slow downward drift after 2022, with the lowest proportions observed in mid-2025, suggesting modest but meaningful improvement in early identification and treatment of high-risk pregnancies.

The combined trend suggests that while haemoglobin testing coverage is being maintained, anaemia remains a significant clinical burden, particularly in the mild category. Persistent severe anaemia cases- though smaller in proportion -continue to highlight gaps in nutritional supplementation and early ANC uptake. Strengthening iron–folate adherence, malaria prevention in pregnancy, and timely ANC initiation can be essential to further reduce the proportion of severe cases."}],[{"type":"text","markdown":"# Testing and Anaemia Status at 36 weeks
Haemoglobin testing at ≥36 weeks of pregnancy remained high across the period, generally ranging between 110,000 and 145,000 tests per quarter. Although this indicates continuity in late pregnancy, testing at 36 weeks remains much lower than at registration, indicating gaps at follow-up testing in late pregnancy. 

Mild to moderate anaemia at 36 weeks consistently accounted for the majority of anaemia detected late in pregnancy. Across the years, it ranged between 42,000 and 52,000 cases per quarter, representing approximately 35%–40% of all women tested. The trend shows a gentle rise from 2021 to 2023 before stabilizing around the mid-40,000s in 2024–2025.

Severe anaemia at 36 weeks remained low in absolute numbers, generally between 5,000 and 8,000 cases per quarter. This corresponds to roughly 4%–6% of all women tested. The proportion peaks around 2021–2022, then gradually decreases through 2024–2025, suggesting small but encouraging improvements in maternal iron status and management of conditions that drive severe anaemia (e.g., malaria in pregnancy, late ANC initiation, nutritional deficiencies).","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"success","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":false,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Hemoglobin Checking and Anaemia Status"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Data Completeness Analysis: Ghana Health Facility Reporting (Jan 2020 - Sep 2025)

## Overall Performance Summary

Data completeness across Ghana's health facilities shows **moderate to good performance** (ranging from 64% to 95%), but reveals significant disparities between regions and indicators that require urgent attention.

## Poorly Performing Indicators

### Critical Gap: Haemoglobin Testing at ≥36 Weeks
This indicator shows **consistently poor performance nationwide** (64-87% completeness):

**Worst Performers:**
- **Savannah: 63.6%** - Critical underperformance
- **Upper West: 68.3%** - Requires immediate intervention
- **North East: 69.5%** - Below acceptable threshold
- **Eastern: 70.1%** - Significant gap
- **Western North: 71.1%** - Concerning

This represents a **systemic weakness** in late-pregnancy monitoring across all regions, with northern regions particularly affected.

### Secondary Concern: ANC Registration Haemoglobin Testing
While better than late-pregnancy testing, several regions fall below 85%:
- **North East: 80.3%**
- **Savannah: 80.2%**
- **Eastern: 82.1%**
- **Upper West: 82.1%**

- **Ahafo**: 87-95% across all indicators (most consistent)
- **Bono**: 86-94% (strong performer)
- **Bono East**: 83-92% (reliable)
- **Northern**: 78-92% (good ANC visits, weaker testing)

## Key Patterns & Insights

### 1. **North-South Divide in Laboratory Testing**
Northern regions (Savannah, Upper West, North East) struggle significantly with haemoglobin testing, suggesting:
- Laboratory capacity constraints
- Supply chain issues for testing materials
- Possible training gaps

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"2e9e2795-d7bd-47a9-a8e8-cd117a7312c5","moduleId":"m003","isDefault":false,"selectedReplicantValue":""}}],[{"type":"text","markdown":"The trend of reported maternal deaths from Q1 2020 to Q3 2025 shows a clear rise followed by a gradual decline:

Upward trend (2020 – 2022): Maternal deaths increased sharply from 532 in Q1 2020 to a peak of 920 – 929 between Q4 2022 and Q2 2023, indicating a worsening pattern through the pandemic and early post-pandemic recovery period.

Peak stability (2022 – 2023): The plateau around 900 deaths per quarter in 2022–2023 suggests sustained high mortality, possibly linked to increased service utilization (more deliveries in facilities) combined with health system strain or reporting improvements.

Gradual decline (2024 – 2025): Deaths began to drop steadily from Q4 2023 (889) to Q2 2025 (683), with a slight uptick to 724 in Q3 2025. This overall 20–25% reduction from the 2022–2023 peak points to gradual improvements in maternal outcomes, likely from strengthened facility readiness and better quality of care.","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"success","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":false,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false}]]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"span":4,"markdown":"The chart shows disruptions and surpluses in national service volume for Polio vaccination (2020–2025).

Between 2020 and early 2021, both Polio 1 and Polio 3 vaccinations remained fairly stable, with only minor fluctuations. However, mid-2022 recorded sharp drops in service volume, visible as deep red disruptions — indicating periods of significant underperformance or interruptions in immunization delivery. These disruptions coincide with supply chain interruptions and reduced facility activity, which likely affected vaccine access.
From late 2022 through 2023, there was a clear rebound — service volumes surged back to pre-disruption levels, showing recovery in immunization delivery. The pattern then stabilized from 2024 onward, maintaining moderate variation without major spikes or dips.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"span":4,"markdown":"# Key Insights:

The chart tracks national-level fluctuations in BCG, Penta1, and Penta3 vaccine service volumes over time.

BCG shows the highest variability, with recurrent service disruptions (highlighted in red) between 2021 and 2024, followed by gradual stabilization in 2025. These dips likely reflect stockouts, delayed campaigns, or population denominator inconsistencies.

Penta1 and Penta3 trends remain relatively stable, with only minor disruptions, suggesting routine immunization delivery was better sustained for these vaccines compared to BCG.

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"span":4,"markdown":"# 🩺 Key findings

# Overall trend
Outpatient (OPD) attendance shows a steady long-term upward trajectory, with visits rising from roughly 2.3 million in 2020 to around 3.4 million by 2025, reflecting progressive recovery and expansion of routine service use.

Disruptions: Major declines in early 2020 and mid-2022 align with national or global health-system slowdowns, likely from COVID-19 restrictions and subsequent resource reallocation. Shorter dips reappear in late 2022 and mid-2023, suggesting intermittent reporting or access barriers.

Surpluses: Noticeable surges in 2021 and early 2024 correspond to post-disruption rebounds, possibly intensified outreach, restored facility operations, or backlogged consultations being cleared."},{"type":"figure","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":true,"figureAdditionalScale":0.8,"presentationObjectInReportInfo":{"id":"56d1a0a2-a8a2-4544-a6fc-fef20cc852cd","moduleId":"m003","selectedReplicantValue":"","isDefault":false},"hideFigureCaption":true,"hideFigureSubCaption":true,"hideFigureFootnote":true}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Surpluses and Disruptions in OPD services"}}
